topic.title: HTML Semantic Elements - Supporting Regions
topic.description: The aside and footer elements mark supplementary and closing regions with built-in ARIA landmarks.

text.value: Beyond the primary content area, every page contains regions that support or close the main experience. HTML5 provides two dedicated elements for these roles:
for content that is tangentially related to the surrounding content, and
for closing information about its nearest sectioning ancestor. Both elements are
to assistive technologies, allowing screen reader users to jump directly to these regions without tabbing through the entire page. Their correct use is specified in the

rows: Sidebars, related links, pull quotes, ads, author bios
Copyright, contact info, legal links, tag lists, author info

content.title: Page-level vs Scoped Landmarks
content.text: A footer that is a direct descendant of body exposes the ARIA contentinfo landmark role. A footer nested inside an article or section is scoped to that ancestor and does not expose contentinfo, so multiple footers on one page are valid as long as only one is at the page level.

examples.title: Page Layout with aside and footer
examples.description: A complete page showing aside used as a sidebar alongside main content, and a page-level footer closing the document. This is the most common real-world pattern for both elements.

examples.output: The browser renders a dark header with 'Dev Weekly', a two-column layout below with the article on the left and a light grey sidebar on the right showing related links and a newsletter prompt. A dark footer spans the full width at the bottom with copyright text and three links. Screen readers expose the sidebar as a 'complementary' landmark and the footer as a 'contentinfo' landmark.

examples.title: Scoped footer and aside Inside article
examples.description: Demonstrates that both aside and footer can be scoped inside an article element, where they apply to that article rather than the whole page, enabling rich self-contained content blocks.
examples.code_snippet:
Why Semantic HTML Matters

By Sarah Chen | 20 Jan 2024

Semantic HTML communicates meaning to browsers, assistive technologies, and search engines...

"Semantic elements replace hundreds of aria-role attributes with a single descriptive tag."

Using the right element for the right job reduces the need for ARIA overrides...

Tags: HTML, Accessibility, Semantics

Last updated: 22 Jan 2024

Copyright 2024 Dev Blog
examples.output: The page shows an article card with a heading and byline, body text, a blue left-bordered pull quote block, more body text, and a small grey tag and date line at the bottom of the card. A simple copyright footer appears below. Screen readers treat the pull quote aside as complementary content scoped to the article, and only the page-level footer is announced as the contentinfo landmark.

examples.title: WRONG vs RIGHT: Misusing aside and footer
examples.description: Shows the two most common misuse patterns for aside and footer and contrasts them with correct usage, explaining what breaks in each case.

examples.output: The WRONG aside example causes screen readers to announce 'Web Design' and 'SEO Services' as supplementary complementary regions rather than core service descriptions, misleading users about the importance of the content. The WRONG footer example creates two contentinfo landmarks that screen readers list separately, confusing users about which footer is authoritative.

text.value: when the content enriches but is not essential to the surrounding content, such as related links, pull quotes, author bios, or advertisement blocks.
at the page level for site-wide closing information like copyright, contact links, and legal notices, and inside
for scoped closing metadata like tags, publish dates, and author credits.
Use both elements when you need assistive technology users to navigate directly to
landmark regions without tabbing through the entire page.
for content that is a core part of the page's primary message. Use
instead when the content is integral to the page narrative.
Avoid placing more than one page-level
landmarks that confuse screen reader users.
, as double-nesting complementary landmarks produces an illogical document outline with no meaningful hierarchy for assistive technologies.

landmark for users to jump to or skip.

as the margin notes in a textbook. They add context and references that enrich the main text, but you can read the chapter completely without them. The core argument lives in the main column.
as the colophon at the back of a book. It tells you who published it, when, under what terms, and how to reach them. It closes and credits the work without being part of the story.
are like the index tabs on a binder. A screen reader user can flip directly to the complementary section or the closing info tab without leafing through every page in between.

subtopics.title: HTML Aside Element
subtopics.description: The aside element marks tangentially related content that is not essential to the main content flow.

text.value: to the nearest sectioning ancestor. At the page level it is typically rendered as a sidebar. Inside an
it is commonly used for pull quotes, author notes, or glossary terms. The element automatically maps to the
, which assistive technologies expose as a navigable landmark. When more than one
exists on a page, each should have a unique
so users can distinguish between them.

content.title: aside is not a generic sidebar container
content.text: Only place content inside aside when removing it would leave the main content fully intact and understandable. If the content is necessary to the page's argument or story, it belongs in main, section, or article instead.

examples.title: Page-level Sidebar and In-article Pull Quote
examples.description: Shows aside used in two distinct contexts: as a page-level sidebar with navigation and subscription links, and as an in-article pull quote, illustrating how the same element adapts to its sectioning ancestor.

examples.output: The page displays a two-column layout. The left column shows the article with a heading, two paragraphs of text, and a blue left-bordered pull quote block between them. The right column shows a light grey sidebar with two sections: Related Articles and Tools, each listing links. Screen readers expose the sidebar as a 'Related resources complementary' landmark and the pull quote as a 'Pull quote complementary' region scoped to the article.

text.value: as the footnotes in an academic paper. They provide extra context, citations, or tangential observations, but the paper's argument stands completely on its own without them. If the content would leave a hole in the main text when removed, it is not an aside.
is like a 'skip to sidebar' sign on a highway. Assistive technology users can take the exit to explore supplementary content and return to the main road whenever they choose.
role, open Chrome DevTools, select the element, switch to the Accessibility tab, and confirm the role reads 'complementary'. If it reads 'generic', the element may be overridden by an explicit

subtopics.title: HTML Footer Element
subtopics.description: The footer element closes its nearest sectioning ancestor, from page-level to article-level content.

text.value: element represents the closing region of its nearest sectioning ancestor:
that is a direct child of
, a navigable page-level landmark. When nested inside
and is instead scoped as closing metadata for that ancestor. A

content.title: footer can contain a nav element
content.text: Wrapping footer navigation links inside a nav element with an aria-label such as aria-label='Footer' creates a named navigation landmark, allowing screen reader users to jump to footer links directly rather than tabbing through the entire page.

examples.title: Page-level Footer with Navigation and Legal Links
examples.description: A production-ready page-level footer showing copyright, secondary navigation links, and social links all correctly structured inside a single footer element.

examples.output: The page shows a heading and content area above a wide dark footer. The footer contains three columns: Product, Company, and Legal, each listing relevant links. A dividing line separates the columns from a centred copyright notice at the very bottom. Screen readers expose the footer as the 'contentinfo' landmark and the two nav elements inside it as 'Product links navigation' and 'Company links navigation'.

text.value: is like the final page of a contract. It does not add new arguments but closes the document with the parties' names, dates, terms, and contact details. Everything needed to act on the document is there, separate from the body of the agreement.
is like the byline and source citation at the end of a newspaper column. It closes that specific story with authorship and context, without claiming to represent the whole newspaper.

or another sectioning element by mistake, it will not expose
and the landmark will be missing from the page.

seo_metadata.meta_title: HTML Semantic Supporting Regions | aside and footer Elements Guide
seo_metadata.meta_description: Learn how to use the HTML aside and footer elements to structure supplementary and closing regions with correct ARIA landmark roles for accessibility and SEO.
